in [1930](/wiki/1930_British_Empire_Games "1930 British Empire Games"), the event has evolved through several name changes, reflecting the changing geopolitical landscape and gradual decolonisation of the Empire. It was known as the British Empire Games until [1950](/wiki/1950_British_Empire_Games "1950 British Empire Games"), the **British Empire and Commonwealth Games** until [1966](/wiki/1966_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1966 British Empire and Commonwealth Games"), and the **British Commonwealth Games** until [1974](/wiki/1974_British_Commonwealth_Games "1974 British Commonwealth Games"). Since the [1978](/wiki/1978_Commonwealth_Games "1978 Commonwealth Games") edition, the event has been officially known as the Commonwealth Games, a name that reflects its modern identity while maintaining its historic connection to Britain. The games are also referred to as the **Friendly Games**.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)

The **Commonwealth Games**[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a quadrennial international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") that brings together athletes from across the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), a political association comprising the majority of former territories of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). First held as the **British Empire Games** in [1930](/wiki/1930_British_Empire_Games "1930 British Empire Games"), the event has evolved through several name changes, reflecting the changing geopolitical landscape and gradual decolonisation of the Empire. It was known as the British Empire Games until [1950](/wiki/1950_British_Empire_Games "1950 British Empire Games"), the **British Empire and Commonwealth Games** until [1966](/wiki/1966_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1966 British Empire and Commonwealth Games"), and the **British Commonwealth Games** until [1974](/wiki/1974_British_Commonwealth_Games "1974 British Commonwealth Games"). Since the [1978](/wiki/1978_Commonwealth_Games "1978 Commonwealth Games") edition, the event has been officially known as the Commonwealth Games, a name that reflects its modern identity while maintaining its historic connection to Britain. The games are also referred to as the **Friendly Games**.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)

The Games are overseen by the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") (CGF), which determines the [sporting programme](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_sports "Commonwealth Games sports") and awards hosting rights. The event upholds its unique traditions, such as the [King's Baton Relay](/wiki/King%27s_Baton_Relay "King's Baton Relay") and the ceremonial hoisting of the Commonwealth Games flag. It is distinguished from other international sporting competitions by its inclusivity and heritage. Athletes with a disability have been integrated as full team members since 2002, and in 2018, the Games became the first global multi-sport competition to offer equal medal events for men and women. By 2022, women's events outnumbered men's for the first time. In addition to mainstream [Olympic sports](/wiki/Olympic_sports "Olympic sports"), the Commonwealth Games includes disciplines such as [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball"), lawn [bowls](/wiki/Bowls "Bowls") and [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), which enjoy particular popularity within the Commonwealth.

The origins of the Games lie in the [Inter-Empire Championships](/wiki/Festival_of_the_Empire#Inter-Empire_Championships "Festival of the Empire") of 1911, with [Melville Marks Robinson](/wiki/Melville_Marks_Robinson "Melville Marks Robinson") playing a pivotal role in establishing the first official Games in [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), Canada. Over time, associated events such as the [Commonwealth Youth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Youth_Games "Commonwealth Youth Games") and the now-defunct [Commonwealth Paraplegic Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "Commonwealth Paraplegic Games") have further expanded participation. Unlike the Olympics and other global competitions, the Commonwealth Games permit representation from fifteen [Commonwealth Games Associations](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Association "Commonwealth Games Association") that are not sovereign states. These include the four [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations") of the United Kingdom, the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"), several [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island") (Australia) and [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue") (New Zealand). Notably, despite being non-sovereign, [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda_at_the_Olympics "Bermuda at the Olympics"), [British Virgin Islands](/wiki/British_Virgin_Islands_at_the_Olympics "British Virgin Islands at the Olympics") and [Cayman Islands](/wiki/Cayman_Islands_at_the_Olympics "Cayman Islands at the Olympics") are recognised as independent National Olympic Committees by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee").

To date, twenty cities in nine countries have hosted the Games. [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") leads with five editions, while both [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland") and [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") have hosted twice. [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") will become the third city to do so, having held the Games in 2014 and again in [2026](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games"). The most recent edition was held in [Birmingham](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games") in 2022. Originally, the [2026 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games") were awarded to [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)") in Australia, but cost concerns led to the state's withdrawal. Subsequently, Glasgow was confirmed as the replacement host city. The upcoming Games will be markedly smaller in scale, featuring only ten sports. As part of a settlement agreement, the Victorian government will provide over £2 million to the Glasgow organisers.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9) The next edition of the Games, the twenty-third or *XXIII*, is scheduled to be held in Glasgow from 23 July to 2 August 2026, while the immediate future of the Games was secured with the announcement in 2025 that the [2030 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2030_Commonwealth_Games "2030 Commonwealth Games") (which will be both the XXIV Games and the Centenary Games) has been award to [Ahmedabad](/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad"), India.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

A sporting competition bringing together the members of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") was first proposed by John Astley Cooper in 1891, five years before the first modern [first modern Olympic Games](/wiki/1896_Summer_Olympics "1896 Summer Olympics"), who wrote letters and articles for several periodicals suggesting a "Pan Brittanic, Pan [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") Contest every four years as a means of increasing goodwill and understanding of the British Empire."[[10]](#cite_note-ANETT-11) John Astley Cooper Committees were formed in Australia, [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") to promote the idea and inspired [Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin") to start the international [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") movement.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

In 1911, an Inter-Empire Championship was held alongside the [Festival of Empire](/wiki/Festival_of_Empire "Festival of Empire"), at [The Crystal Palace](/wiki/The_Crystal_Palace "The Crystal Palace") in [London](/wiki/London "London") to celebrate the [coronation of George V](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_V_and_Mary "Coronation of George V and Mary"), and were championed by [The Earl of Plymouth](/wiki/Ivor_Windsor-Clive,_2nd_Earl_of_Plymouth "Ivor Windsor-Clive, 2nd Earl of Plymouth") and [Lord Desborough](/wiki/William_Grenfell,_1st_Baron_Desborough "William Grenfell, 1st Baron Desborough").[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) Teams from Australasia (Australia and New Zealand), Canada, South Africa, and the United Kingdom competed in events for athletics, boxing, swimming and wrestling.[[15]](#cite_note-16) Canada won the championships and was presented with a silver cup (gifted by [Lord Lonsdale](/wiki/Earl_of_Lonsdale "Earl of Lonsdale")) which was 2 feet 6 inches (76 cm) high and weighed 340 ounces (9.6 kg). A correspondent of the *[Auckland Star](/wiki/Auckland_Star "Auckland Star")* criticised the Games, calling them a "grievous disappointment" that were "not worthy of the title of 'Empire Sports'".[[16]](#cite_note-17)

While planning for the [1928 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics") in Amsterdam, [Amateur Athletic Union of Canada](/wiki/Amateur_Athletic_Union_of_Canada "Amateur Athletic Union of Canada") executive [J. Howard Crocker](/wiki/J._Howard_Crocker "J. Howard Crocker") spoke with journalist [Melville Marks Robinson](/wiki/Melville_Marks_Robinson "Melville Marks Robinson") of *[The Hamilton Spectator](/wiki/The_Hamilton_Spectator "The Hamilton Spectator")*, about hosting an international sporting event in Canada. Robinson proposed and lobbied to host what became the [British Empire Games](/wiki/1930_British_Empire_Games "1930 British Empire Games") in Hamilton, Ontario, in 1930.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-TOP-19) Robinson then served as the manager of the Canadian track and field team for the 1930 British Empire Games.[[18]](#cite_note-TOP-19)

Although there are 56 sovereign states that are members of the Commonwealth of Nations, there are 74 active [Commonwealth Games Associations](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Associations "Commonwealth Games Associations").[[19]](#cite_note-20)They are divided into six regions (Africa, the Americas, the Caribbean, Europe, Asia and Oceania) and each has a similar function to the [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committee "National Olympic Committee") in relation with their countries or territories. In some, like [India](/wiki/India "India") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), the CGA functions are assumed by their NOCs.

Only six national federations have participated in every Commonwealth Games: Australia, Canada, England, New Zealand, [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). Of these six, Australia, England, Canada and New Zealand have each won at least one gold medal in every Games. Australia has been the highest-achieving team for thirteen editions of the Games, England for seven and Canada for one. These three teams also top the all-time Commonwealth Games medal table in that order.

### Editions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Editions")]

#### British Empire Games (1930-1950)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: British Empire Games (1930-1950)")]

The [1930 British Empire Games](/wiki/1930_British_Empire_Games "1930 British Empire Games") were the first of what later became known as the Commonwealth Games, and was held in [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), Canada from 16 to 23 August 1930 and opened by [Lord Willingdon](/wiki/Freeman_Freeman-Thomas,_1st_Marquess_of_Willingdon "Freeman Freeman-Thomas, 1st Marquess of Willingdon").[[20]](#cite_note-H30-21) Eleven countries: Australia, [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), [British Guyana](/wiki/British_Guiana "British Guiana"), Canada, England, [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), [Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland"), New Zealand, [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), South Africa and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), sent a total of 400 athletes to compete in athletics, boxing, lawn bowls, rowing, swimming and diving and wrestling. The opening and closing ceremonies as well as athletics took place at [Civic Stadium](/wiki/Ivor_Wynne_Stadium "Ivor Wynne Stadium").[[21]](#cite_note-E30-22) The cost of the Games were $97,973.[[21]](#cite_note-E30-22) Women competed in only the aquatic events.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Canadian triple jumper [Gordon Smallacombe](/wiki/Gordon_Smallacombe "Gordon Smallacombe") won the first ever gold medal in the history of the Games.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/SLNSW_23801_Opening_Empire_Games_Sydney_Cricket_Ground.jpg/250px-SLNSW_23801_Opening_Empire_Games_Sydney_Cricket_Ground.jpg)](/wiki/File:SLNSW_23801_Opening_Empire_Games_Sydney_Cricket_Ground.jpg)

Opening ceremony of the [1938 British Empire Games](/wiki/1938_British_Empire_Games "1938 British Empire Games") at the [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground").

The [1934 British Empire Games](/wiki/1934_British_Empire_Games "1934 British Empire Games") were the second of what is now known as the Commonwealth Games, held in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England. The host city was London, with the main venue at [Wembley Park](/wiki/Wembley_Park "Wembley Park"), although the track cycling events were in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"). The 1934 Games had originally been awarded to [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg"), but was given to London instead because of serious concerns about prejudice against Asian and black athletes in South Africa. The affiliation of Irish athletes at the 1934 Games representation [remains unclear](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_British_Empire_Games#§1934_games "Ireland at the British Empire Games") but there was no official [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") team. Sixteen national teams took part, including new participants [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Colony_of_Jamaica "Colony of Jamaica"), [Southern Rhodesia](/wiki/Southern_Rhodesia "Southern Rhodesia") and [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago").[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The [1938 British Empire Games](/wiki/1938_British_Empire_Games "1938 British Empire Games") were the third British Empire Games, which was held in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia. It was timed to coincide with Sydney's sesqui-centenary (150 years since the foundation of British settlement in Australia). Held in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") for the first time, the III Games opening ceremony took place at the famed [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground") in front of 40,000 spectators. Fifteen nations participated down under at the Sydney Games involving a total of 464 athletes and 43 officials. [Fiji](/wiki/Colony_of_Fiji "Colony of Fiji") and [Ceylon](/wiki/Dominion_of_Ceylon "Dominion of Ceylon") made their debuts. Seven sports were featured in the Sydney Games – athletics, boxing, cycling, lawn bowls, rowing, swimming and diving and wrestling.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

The [1950 British Empire Games](/wiki/1950_British_Empire_Games "1950 British Empire Games") were the fourth edition and were held in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), New Zealand, after a twelve-year gap from the third edition of the games. The fourth games were originally awarded to [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Canada and was to be held in 1942, but was cancelled due to the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). The opening ceremony at [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park") was attended by 40,000 spectators, while nearly 250,000 people attended the Auckland Games. Twelve countries sent a total of 590 athletes to Auckland. [Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya") and [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") made their first appearances.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

#### British Empire and Commonwealth Games (1954-1966)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: British Empire and Commonwealth Games (1954-1966)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Bannister_and_Landy.jpg/250px-Bannister_and_Landy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bannister_and_Landy.jpg)

Statue in Vancouver commemorating the "Miracle Mile" between [Roger Bannister](/wiki/Roger_Bannister "Roger Bannister") and [John Landy](/wiki/John_Landy "John Landy")

The fifth edition of the Games, the [1954 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1954_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1954 British Empire and Commonwealth Games"), were held in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), Canada. This was the first event since the name change from British Empire Games took effect in 1952, the same year of Queen Elizabeth II's reign. The fifth edition of the Games placed Vancouver on a world stage and featured memorable sporting moments as well as outstanding entertainment, technical innovation and cultural events. The 'Miracle Mile', as it became known, saw both the gold medallist, [Roger Bannister](/wiki/Roger_Bannister "Roger Bannister") of England and silver medallist [John Landy](/wiki/John_Landy "John Landy") of Australia, run sub-four-minute races in an event that was televised live across the world for the first time. [Northern Rhodesia](/wiki/Northern_Rhodesia "Northern Rhodesia") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") made their debuts and both performed well, winning eight and six medals respectively.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/1958_Commonwealth_Games_3d_Stamp.jpg/250px-1958_Commonwealth_Games_3d_Stamp.jpg)](/wiki/File:1958_Commonwealth_Games_3d_Stamp.jpg)

3 pence British stamp with theme of [1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1958_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games"), [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), Wales

The [1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1958_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games") were held in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), Wales. The sixth edition of the games marked the largest sporting event ever held in Wales and it was the smallest country ever to host a British Empire and Commonwealth Games. [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") had to wait twelve years longer than originally scheduled to become host of the Games, as the 1946 event was cancelled because of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). The Cardiff Games introduced the [Queen's Baton Relay](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "Queen's Baton Relay"), which has been conducted as a prelude to every British Empire and Commonwealth Games ever since. Thirty-five nations sent a total of 1,122 athletes and 228 officials to the Cardiff Games and 23 countries and dependencies won medals, including for the first time, [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya_Colony "Kenya Colony") and the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man").[[28]](#cite_note-29) In the run up to the Cardiff games, many leading sports stars including [Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews"), [Jimmy Hill](/wiki/Jimmy_Hill "Jimmy Hill") and [Don Revie](/wiki/Don_Revie "Don Revie") were signatories in a letter to *The Times* on 17 July 1958 deploring the presence of white-only South African sports, opposing 'the policy of apartheid' in international sport and defending 'the principle of racial equality which is embodied in the Declaration of the Olympic Games'.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

The [1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1962_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games") were held in [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth"), [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia"). Thirty-five countries sent a total of 863 athletes and 178 officials to Perth. [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") was among the medal winners for the first time, while [British Honduras](/wiki/British_Honduras "British Honduras"), [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica"), [Papua and New Guinea](/wiki/Territory_of_Papua_and_New_Guinea "Territory of Papua and New Guinea") and [St Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucia "Saint Lucia") all made their inaugural Games appearances. [Aden](/wiki/Colony_of_Aden "Colony of Aden") also competed by special invitation. [Sarawak](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Sarawak "Crown Colony of Sarawak"), [North Borneo](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_North_Borneo "Crown Colony of North Borneo") and [Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya") competed for the last time, before taking part in 1966 under the [Malaysian flag](/wiki/Malaysian_flag "Malaysian flag"). In addition, [Rhodesia and Nyasaland](/wiki/Federation_of_Rhodesia_and_Nyasaland "Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland") competed in the Games as an entity for the first and only time.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

The [1966 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1966_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1966 British Empire and Commonwealth Games") were held in [Kingston, Jamaica](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"). This was the first time that the Games had been held outside the so-called [White Dominions](/wiki/White_Dominion "White Dominion"). Thirty-four nations (including [South Arabia](/wiki/Federation_of_South_Arabia "Federation of South Arabia")) competed in the [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica") Games, sending a total of 1,316 athletes and officials.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

#### British Commonwealth Games (1970-1974)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: British Commonwealth Games (1970-1974)")]

The [1970 British Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1970_British_Commonwealth_Games "1970 British Commonwealth Games") were held in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), Scotland. This was the first time the name British Commonwealth Games was adopted, the first time [metric units](/wiki/Metric_system "Metric system") rather than [imperial units](/wiki/Imperial_unit "Imperial unit") were used in events, the first time the games were held in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and also the first time that HM Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") attended in her capacity as [Head of the Commonwealth](/wiki/Head_of_the_Commonwealth "Head of the Commonwealth").[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The [1974 British Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1974_British_Commonwealth_Games "1974 British Commonwealth Games") were held in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), New Zealand. The event was officially named *The Friendly Games*, and was also the first edition to feature a theme song. Following the [massacre of Israeli athletes](/wiki/1972_Munich_attack "1972 Munich attack") at the [1972 Munich Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"), the tenth games at Christchurch were the first [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") to place the safety of participants and spectators as its uppermost requirement. Security guards surrounded the athlete's village and there was an exceptionally high-profile police presence. Only 22 countries succeeded in winning medals from the total haul of 374 medals on offer, but first time winners included [Western Samoa](/wiki/Western_Samoa "Western Samoa"), [Lesotho](/wiki/Lesotho "Lesotho") and Swaziland (since 2018 named [Eswatini](/wiki/Eswatini "Eswatini")).[[33]](#cite_note-34) The theme song for the 1974 British Commonwealth Games was called "Join Together".

#### Commonwealth Games (1978-present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Commonwealth Games (1978-present)")]

The [1978 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1978_Commonwealth_Games "1978 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), Canada. This event was the first to bear the current day name of the Commonwealth Games, and also marked a new high as almost 1,500 athletes from 46 countries took part. They were boycotted by [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") in protest against [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand")'s sporting contacts with [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid")-era [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), as well as by [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda") in protest at alleged Canadian hostilities toward the government of [Idi Amin](/wiki/Idi_Amin "Idi Amin").[[34]](#cite_note-MacintoshHawes1994-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Commonwealth_Games_Opening_Ceremony_-_Brisbane_1982.jpg/250px-Commonwealth_Games_Opening_Ceremony_-_Brisbane_1982.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commonwealth_Games_Opening_Ceremony_-_Brisbane_1982.jpg)

Opening ceremony of the [1982 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1982_Commonwealth_Games "1982 Commonwealth Games") at [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), Australia

The [1982 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1982_Commonwealth_Games "1982 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), Australia. Forty-six nations participated in the [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") Games with a new record total of 1,583 athletes and 571 officials. As hosts, Australia headed the medal table leading the way ahead of England, Canada, Scotland and New Zealand respectively. [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") made its first appearance at the Games, having earlier competed as [Southern Rhodesia](/wiki/Southern_Rhodesia "Southern Rhodesia") and as part of [Rhodesia and Nyasaland](/wiki/Federation_of_Rhodesia_and_Nyasaland "Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland").[[36]](#cite_note-37) The theme song for the 1982 Commonwealth Games was called "You're Here To Win".

The [1986 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), Scotland and were the second Games to be held in Edinburgh. Participation at the 1986 Games was affected by a boycott by 32 African, Asian and Caribbean nations in protest at British Prime Minister [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher")'s refusal to condemn sporting contacts of apartheid era South Africa in 1985, but the Games rebounded and continued to grow thereafter. Twenty-six nations did attend the second Edinburgh Games, and sent a total of 1,662 athletes and 461 officials.[[37]](#cite_note-38) The theme song for the 1986 Commonwealth Games was called "Spirit Of Youth".

The [1990 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1990_Commonwealth_Games "1990 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), New Zealand. They were the fourteenth Commonwealth Games, the third to be hosted by [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "New Zealand at the Commonwealth Games") and Auckland's second. A new record of 55 nations participated in the second Auckland Games, sending 2,826 athletes and officials.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Pakistan returned to the Commonwealth in 1989 after withdrawing in 1972, and competed in the 1990 Games after an absence of twenty years.[[39]](#cite_note-40) The theme song for the 1990 Commonwealth Games was called "This Is The Moment".

The [1994 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1994_Commonwealth_Games "1994 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), Canada. This event was the fourth to take place in Canada. The games marked another point of [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa")'s return to the sporting atmosphere following the [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") era, and over thirty years since the country last competed in the [Games in 1958](/wiki/1958_Commonwealth_Games "1958 Commonwealth Games"). [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") made its Commonwealth Games debut following its independence from South Africa in 1990. It was also Hong Kong's last appearance at the games before the [transfer of sovereignty](/wiki/Transfer_of_sovereignty_over_Hong_Kong "Transfer of sovereignty over Hong Kong") from Britain to China. Sixty-three nations sent 2,557 athletes and 914 officials.[[40]](#cite_note-41) The theme song for the 1994 Commonwealth Games was called "Let Your Spirit Take Flight".

The [1998 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1998_Commonwealth_Games "1998 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), Malaysia. For the first time in its 68-year history, the Commonwealth Games were held in Asia. The event was also the first Games to feature team sports (cricket, rugby 7s, netball and field hockey) along ten pin bowling and squash– an overwhelming success that added large numbers to both participant and TV audience numbers. A new record of 70 countries sent a total of 5,065 athletes and officials to the Kuala Lumpur Games. The top five countries in the medal standing were Australia, England, Canada, Malaysia (who made their best games' performance until that date) and South Africa. [Nauru](/wiki/Nauru "Nauru") also achieved an impressive haul of three gold medals. [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon"), [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati") and [Tuvalu](/wiki/Tuvalu "Tuvalu") debuted.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The theme song for the 1998 Commonwealth Games was called "Forever As One".

#### During the 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: During the 21st century")]

The [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England. The event was hosted in England for the first time since 1934 and hosted to coincide with the [Golden Jubilee of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Golden_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Golden Jubilee of Elizabeth II"), head of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). In terms of sports and events, the 2002 event was until the 2010 edition the largest Commonwealth Games in history featuring 281 events across 17 sports. The final medal tally was led by Australia, followed by host England and Canada. The 2002 Commonwealth Games had set a new benchmark for hosting the Commonwealth Games and for cities wishing to bid for them with a heavy emphasis on legacy.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The theme song for the 2002 Commonwealth Games was called "[Where My Heart Will Take Me](/wiki/Faith_of_the_Heart "Faith of the Heart")".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/MSC%2C_2006_Commonwealth_Games.jpg/250px-MSC%2C_2006_Commonwealth_Games.jpg)](/wiki/File:MSC,_2006_Commonwealth_Games.jpg)

Athletics at the [Melbourne Cricket Ground](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground") (MCG) during the [2006 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne")

The [2006 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Victoria, Australia](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia"). The only difference between the [2006 games](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games") and the [2002 games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") was the absence of [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"), which withdrew from the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") after being suspended by the organization in 2002.[[43]](#cite_note-44) For the first time in the history of the Games the [Queen's Baton](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "Queen's Baton Relay") visited every single Commonwealth nation and territory taking part in the Games, a journey of 180,000 kilometres (110,000 mi). Over 4000 athletes took part in the sporting competitions. Again the Top 3 on the medal table is Australia, followed by England and Canada.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The theme song for the 2006 Commonwealth Games was called "Together We Are One".

The [2010 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2010_Commonwealth_Games "2010 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), India. The Games cost $11 billion and were the most expensive Commonwealth Games ever. It was the first time that the Commonwealth Games was held in India, also the first time that a [Commonwealth republic](/wiki/Republics_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations "Republics in the Commonwealth of Nations") hosted the games and the second time it was held in Asia after Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia in 1998. A total of 6,081 athletes from 71 [Commonwealth nations and dependencies](/wiki/The_Commonwealth "The Commonwealth") competed in 21 sports and 272 events. The final [medal tally](/wiki/2010_Commonwealth_Games_medal_table "2010 Commonwealth Games medal table") was led by [Australia](/wiki/Australia_at_the_2010_Commonwealth_Games "Australia at the 2010 Commonwealth Games"). The host nation India achieved its [best performance ever in any sporting event](/wiki/India_at_the_2010_Commonwealth_Games "India at the 2010 Commonwealth Games"), finishing second overall.[[45]](#cite_note-46) [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") made its Games debut.[[46]](#cite_note-47) The theme song for the 2010 Commonwealth Games was called "Live, Rise, Ascend, Win".

The [2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Scotland. These games were the largest multi-sport event ever held in Scotland with around 4,950 athletes from 71 different nations and territories competing in 18 different sports, outranking the 1970 and 1986 Commonwealth Games in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), capital city of Scotland. [Usain Bolt](/wiki/Usain_Bolt "Usain Bolt") competed in the [4×100 metres relay](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Athletics at the 2014 Commonwealth Games") of the 2014 Commonwealth Games and set a [Commonwealth Games record](/wiki/List_of_Commonwealth_Games_records_in_athletics#Men.27s_records "List of Commonwealth Games records in athletics") with his teammates.[[47]](#cite_note-48) The Games received acclaim for their organisation, attendance, and the public enthusiasm of the people of Scotland, with the CGF chief executive Mike Hooper hailing them as "the standout games in the history of the movement".[[48]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Commonwealth_Cycling_%28geograph_4105477%29.jpg/250px-Commonwealth_Cycling_%28geograph_4105477%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commonwealth_Cycling_(geograph_4105477).jpg)

Cycling event during the [2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games") hosted in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Scotland

The [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), Australia, the fifth time Australia hosted the Games. There were an equal number of events for men and women, the first time in history that a major multi-sport event had equality in terms of events.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)

The [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games") were held in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), England. It was the third Commonwealth Games to be hosted in England, following [London 1934](/wiki/1934_British_Empire_Games "1934 British Empire Games") and [Manchester 2002](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games").[[51]](#cite_note-52) The 2022 Commonwealth Games coincided with the [Platinum Jubilee of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Platinum_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Platinum Jubilee of Elizabeth II") and the tenth anniversary of the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") and the [2012 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics "2012 Summer Paralympics"), both staged in [London](/wiki/London "London"). The 2022 Commonwealth Games was the last edition to be held under Queen Elizabeth II, before her [death](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Elizabeth_II "Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II") on 8 September 2022.

On 16 February 2022, it was announced that the [2026 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games") would be held for a record sixth time in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), but for the first time they would be decentralised, as the state of [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") signed as host 'city'. The event were to have four regional clusters mainly focused in [Bendigo](/wiki/Bendigo "Bendigo") region, and another three regional centres. However, in July 2023, the Victorian Premier [Daniel Andrews](/wiki/Daniel_Andrews "Daniel Andrews") announced that Victoria would no longer host the 2026 Games.[[52]](#cite_note-:2-53) The Scottish government later agreed to hold the 2026 games in Glasgow, following Victoria's cancellation, however the games will be "scaled down" with only 10 sports being staged in four venues, and a commitment that public funds would not be required.[[53]](#cite_note-:4-54) The 2026 Commonwealth Games will be the first held under the reign of [King Charles III](/wiki/King_Charles_III "King Charles III").
The three nations to have hosted the Commonwealth Games the most times are Australia (5), Canada (4) and New Zealand (3). With the 2022 games, England increased its number to three. Three games have been hosted in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), one in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), two in Asia ([Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") (1) and [India](/wiki/India "India") (1)) and one in the Caribbean ([Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") (1)).[[54]](#cite_note-:1-55) The event has been awarded to, but never been held in, Africa, with [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") being stripped of the 2022 Games following financial issues.

### Paraplegic Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Paraplegic Games")]

Main article: [Commonwealth Paraplegic Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "Commonwealth Paraplegic Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/1962_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games_Competitors_Team_Photograph.jpg/250px-1962_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games_Competitors_Team_Photograph.jpg)](/wiki/File:1962_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games_Competitors_Team_Photograph.jpg)

Athletes of the [1962 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games](/wiki/1962_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1962 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games") at [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth")

The Commonwealth Paraplegic Games were an international, [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") involving athletes with a disability from the [Commonwealth countries](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). The event was sometimes referred to as the Paraplegic Empire Games and British Commonwealth Paraplegic Games. Athletes were generally those with [spinal injuries](/wiki/Spinal_injuries "Spinal injuries") or [polio](/wiki/Poliomyelitis "Poliomyelitis"). The event was first held in 1962 and disestablished in 1974.[[55]](#cite_note-56) The Games were held in the country hosting the Commonwealth Games for able-bodied athletes. The countries that had hosted the Commonwealth Paraplegic Games were Australia, Jamaica, Scotland and New Zealand in [1962](/wiki/1962_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1962 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games"), [1966](/wiki/1966_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1966 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games"), [1970](/wiki/1970_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1970 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games") and [1974](/wiki/1974_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1974 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games"). Six countries – Australia, England, New Zealand, [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") — had been represented at all Commonwealth Paraplegic Games. Australia and England had been[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the top-ranking nation two times each: [1962](/wiki/1962_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1962 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games"), [1974](/wiki/1974_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1974 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games") and [1966](/wiki/1966_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1966 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games"), [1970](/wiki/1970_Commonwealth_Paraplegic_Games "1970 Commonwealth Paraplegic Games").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Inclusion of disabled athletes in Commonwealth Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Inclusion of disabled athletes in Commonwealth Games")]

Athletes with a disability were then first included the [1994 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1994_Commonwealth_Games "1994 Commonwealth Games") in [Victoria, British Columbia](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia") when this events was added to athletics and lawn bowls,[[56]](#cite_note-57) As at [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") in Manchester, England, they were included as compulsory events, making them the first fully [inclusive](/wiki/Inclusion_(disability_rights) "Inclusion (disability rights)") international multi-sport games. This meant that results were included in the medal count and the athletes are full members of each country delegation.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

During the 2007 General Assembly of the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") (CGF) at [Colombo](/wiki/Colombo "Colombo"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), the [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") (IPC) and CGF signed a co-operative agreement to ensure a formal institutional relationship between the two bodies and secure the future participation of elite athletes with a disability (EAD) in future Commonwealth Games.

Then, IPC President [Philip Craven](/wiki/Philip_Craven "Philip Craven") said during the General Assembly:

> "We look forward to working with CGF to develop the possibilities of athletes with a disability at the Commonwealth Games and within the Commonwealth. This partnership will help to galvanize Paralympic sports development in Commonwealth countries/territories and seek to create and promote greater opportunities in sport for athletes with a disability".

— IPC President [Sir Philip Craven](/wiki/Sir_Philip_Craven "Sir Philip Craven")

The co-operation agreement outlined the strong partnership between the IPC and the CGF. It recognised the IPC as the respective sport body and have the function to oversee the co-ordination and delivery of the Commonwealth Games EAD sports programme and committed both organisations to work together in supporting the growth of the Paralympic and Commonwealth Games Movements.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Winter Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Winter Games")]

Main article: [Commonwealth Winter Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Winter_Games "Commonwealth Winter Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/StMoritz.jpg/250px-StMoritz.jpg)](/wiki/File:StMoritz.jpg)

[St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), the venue for all three Winter Games from 1958 to 1966

The [Commonwealth Winter Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Winter_Games "Commonwealth Winter Games") was a [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") comprising [winter sports](/wiki/Winter_sports "Winter sports"), last held in 1970. Four editions of the Games have been staged. The [Commonwealth Winter Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Winter_Games "Commonwealth Winter Games") were designed as a counterbalance to the Commonwealth Games, which focuses on summer sports, to accompany the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") and [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games"). The winter Games were founded by [T.D. Richardson](/wiki/T.D._Richardson "T.D. Richardson").[[59]](#cite_note-Semanticus-Richardson-60) The 1958 Commonwealth Winter Games were held in [St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), Switzerland and was the inaugural games for the winter edition.[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62) The 1962 Games were also held in St. Moritz, complementing the [1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1962_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games") in [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth"), Australia, and the 1966 event was held in St. Moritz as well, following the 1970 edition, the idea was discontinued.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Youth Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Youth Games")]

Main article: [Commonwealth Youth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Youth_Games "Commonwealth Youth Games")

The [Commonwealth Youth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Youth_Games "Commonwealth Youth Games") is an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") organised by the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation"). The Commonwealth Youth Games is held every four years with the current Commonwealth Games format. The [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") discussed the idea of a Millennium [Commonwealth Youth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Youth_Games "Commonwealth Youth Games") in 1997. In 1998, the concept was agreed on for the purpose of providing a Commonwealth multi-sport event for young people born in 1986 or later. The first version was held in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), Scotland from 10 to 14 August 2000. The age limitation of the athletes is 14 to 18.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

## Commonwealth Games Federation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Commonwealth Games Federation")]

Main article: [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/London_Shopping_002_%286166876525%29_%282%29.jpg/250px-London_Shopping_002_%286166876525%29_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:London_Shopping_002_(6166876525)_(2).jpg)

Headquarters of the [CGF](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") at the Commonwealth House (centre) in London

The [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") (CGF) is the international organisation responsible for the direction and control of the Commonwealth Games and [Commonwealth Youth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Youth_Games "Commonwealth Youth Games"), and is the foremost authority in matters relating to the games.[[64]](#cite_note-65) The Commonwealth House in London, England hosts the headquarters of CGF.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The Commonwealth House also hosts the headquarters of the [Royal Commonwealth Society](/wiki/Royal_Commonwealth_Society "Royal Commonwealth Society") and the [Commonwealth Local Government Forum](/wiki/Commonwealth_Local_Government_Forum "Commonwealth Local Government Forum").[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)

The Commonwealth Games Movement is made of three major elements:

* [International Federations](/wiki/List_of_international_sport_federations "List of international sport federations") (IFs) are the [governing bodies](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body") that supervise a sport at an international level. For example, the International Basketball Federation ([FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA")) is the international governing body for basketball.[[68]](#cite_note-69)
* [Commonwealth Games Associations](/wiki/Category:Commonwealth_Games_Associations "Category:Commonwealth Games Associations") (CGAs) represent and regulate the Commonwealth Games Movement within each country and perform similar functions as the [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committees "National Olympic Committees"). For example, the [Commonwealth Games England](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_England "Commonwealth Games England") (CGE) is the CGA of England. There are currently 72 CGAs recognised by the CGF.[[69]](#cite_note-70)
* Organising Committees for the Commonwealth Games (OCCWGs) are temporary committees responsible for the organisation of each Commonwealth Games. OCCWGs are dissolved after each Games once the final report is delivered to the CGF.

English is the official language of the Commonwealth. The other language used at each Commonwealth Games is the language of the host country (or languages, if a country has more than one official language apart from English). Every proclamation (such as the announcement of each country during the parade of nations in the opening ceremony) is spoken in these two (or more) languages. If the host country does this, it is their responsibility to choose the language{s) and their order.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

## King's Baton Relay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: King's Baton Relay")]

Main article: [King's Baton Relay](/wiki/King%27s_Baton_Relay "King's Baton Relay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Bilston_St.-Garrick_St._-_geograph.org.uk_-_536739.jpg/250px-Bilston_St.-Garrick_St._-_geograph.org.uk_-_536739.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bilston_St.-Garrick_St._-_geograph.org.uk_-_536739.jpg)

The [Queen's Jubilee Baton Relay](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay#Manchester_2002 "Queen's Baton Relay") for the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games")

The King's Baton Relay is a relay around the world held prior to the beginning of the Commonwealth Games. The Baton carries a message from the [Head of the Commonwealth](/wiki/Head_of_the_Commonwealth "Head of the Commonwealth"). The Relay traditionally begins at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") in London as a part of the city's [Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day") festivities. The King entrusts the baton to the first relay runner. At the Opening Ceremony of the Games, the final relay runner hands the baton back to the King or his representative, who reads the message aloud to officially open the Games. The King's Baton Relay is similar to the [Olympic Torch Relay](/wiki/Olympic_Torch_Relay "Olympic Torch Relay").[[71]](#cite_note-RelayTradition-72)

The Relay was introduced at the [1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1958_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1958 British Empire and Commonwealth Games") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), Wales as the Queen's Baton Relay. Up until, and including, the [1994 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1994_Commonwealth_Games "1994 Commonwealth Games"), the Relay only went through England and the host nation. The Relay for the [1998 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1998_Commonwealth_Games "1998 Commonwealth Games") in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), Malaysia was the first to travel to other nations of the Commonwealth.

The [Queen's Baton Relay](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "2018 Commonwealth Games Queen's Baton Relay") for the [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") held on the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), Australia, was the longest in Commonwealth Games history. Covering 230,000 km (150,000 miles) over 388 days, the Baton made its way through the six Commonwealth regions of Africa, the Americas, the Caribbean, Europe, Asia and Oceania. For the first time, the Queen's Baton was presented at the Commonwealth Youth Games during its [sixth edition](/wiki/2017_Commonwealth_Youth_Games "2017 Commonwealth Youth Games") in 2017, which were held in [Nassau](/wiki/Nassau,_Bahamas "Nassau, Bahamas"), [Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas").[[72]](#cite_note-73)

## Ceremonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ceremonies")]

### Opening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Opening")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Comm_Games_2006_finale.JPG/250px-Comm_Games_2006_finale.JPG)](/wiki/File:Comm_Games_2006_finale.JPG)

Opening ceremony of the [2006 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games") at [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne")

The opening ceremony typically starts with the hoisting of the host country's flag and a performance of its national anthem. The flag of the Commonwealth Games Federation, flag of the last hosting nation and the next hosting nation are also hosted during the opening ceremony. The host nation then presents artistic displays of music, singing, dance and theatre representative of its culture. The artistic presentations have grown in scale and complexity as successive hosts attempt to provide a ceremony that outlasts its predecessor's in terms of memorability. The opening ceremony of the Delhi Games reportedly cost $70 million, with much of the cost incurred in the artistic segment.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

After the artistic portion of the ceremony, the athletes parade into the stadium grouped by nation. The last hosting nation is traditionally the first nation to enter. Nations then enter the stadium alphabetical or continental wise with the host country's athletes being the last to enter. Speeches are given, formally opening the Games. Finally, the [King's Baton](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "Queen's Baton Relay") is brought into the stadium and passed on until it reaches the final baton carrier, often a successful Commonwealth athlete from the host nation, who hands it over to the [Head of the Commonwealth](/wiki/Head_of_the_Commonwealth "Head of the Commonwealth") or his representative.

### Closing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Closing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Commonwealth_Games_closing_ceremony_picture.jpeg/250px-Commonwealth_Games_closing_ceremony_picture.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Commonwealth_Games_closing_ceremony_picture.jpeg)

Closing ceremony of the [2010 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2010_Commonwealth_Games "2010 Commonwealth Games") at Delhi

The closing ceremony of the Commonwealth Games takes place after all sporting events have concluded. Flag-bearers from each participating country enter the stadium, followed by the athletes who enter together, without any national distinction. The president of the organising committee and the CGF president make their closing speeches and the Games are officially closed. The CGF president also speaks about the conduct of the games. The mayor of the city that organised the Games transfers the CGF flag to the president of the CGF, who then passes it on to the mayor of the city hosting the next Commonwealth Games. The next host nation then also briefly introduces itself with artistic displays of dance and theatre representative of its culture. Many great artists and singers had performed at the ceremonies of the Commonwealth Games.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

At the closing ceremony of every Commonwealth Games, the CGF President makes an award and presents a trophy to one athlete who has competed with particular distinction and honour both in terms of athletic performance and overall contribution to his or her team. Athletes are nominated by their Commonwealth Games Association at the end of the final day of competition and the winner is selected by a panel comprising the CGF President and representatives from each of the six Commonwealth Regions. The '[David Dixon Award](/wiki/David_Dixon_Award "David Dixon Award")' as it is called was introduced in [Manchester 2002](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games"), after the late David Dixon, former Honorary Secretary of the [CGF](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation"), in honour of his monumental contribution to Commonwealth sport for many years.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

### Medal presentation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Medal presentation")]

A medal ceremony is held after each event is concluded. The winner, second and third-place competitors or teams stand on top of a three-tiered rostrum to be awarded their respective medals. After the medals are given out by a CGF member, the national flags of the three medallists are raised while the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of the gold medallist's country plays. Volunteering citizens of the host country also act as hosts during the medal ceremonies, as they aid the officials who present the medals and act as flag-bearers.

## Commonwealth sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Commonwealth sports")]

Further information: [Commonwealth Games sports](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_sports "Commonwealth Games sports") and [Commonwealth Games records](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_records "Commonwealth Games records")

Unlike other sporting events, the Commonwealth Games have a flexible sporting programme that respects the infrastructure and demands of the host city. This is also reflected in its holding dates, which may vary according to the weather conditions of each host city. Therefore, the programme for each edition could varies. Between 1930 and 1994, only individual events were part of the programme and it was only in 1998 that authorisation was given for the addition of team sports. It is common for each edition since then to have a list of seven to ten mandatory sports that must be played in this edition and must be approved 4 years in advance. Thus, the minimum number of sports per edition is 10 and the maximum is of 17. However, local demands can also increase the number of sports contested. Notable cases are freestyle wrestling in Delhi 2010 and beach volleyball in Gold Coast 2018. Special exceptions can also be made, such as the one in the last edition held in Birmingham, England, in which 3 extra sports were added to the programme.[[81]](#cite_note-82) The current rules also determine gender parity, whereby men and women have an equal (or broadly equal) share of events.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84)

This rule was not exceptionally applied to the 2026 Games, as Glasgow took over as host on an emergency basis. As a result, the program will feature only 10 sports—the fewest number since the 1994 Games:[[84]](#cite_note-BBCSportsOct24-85)

There are a total of 23 sports (with three multi-disciplinary sports) and a ten [para-sports](/wiki/Para-sports "Para-sports") which are approved by the Commonwealth Games Federation.

## Participating nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Participating nations")]

Main article: [List of participating nations at the Commonwealth Games](/wiki/List_of_participating_nations_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "List of participating nations at the Commonwealth Games")

Only six teams have attended every Commonwealth Games: Australia, Canada, England, New Zealand, Scotland, and Wales. Australia has been the highest scoring team for thirteen games, England for seven, and Canada for one.

### Commonwealth nations yet to send teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Commonwealth nations yet to send teams")]

Very few Commonwealth nations and dependencies have yet to take part:[[98]](#cite_note-Commonwealth_of_Nations_2014-123)[[99]](#cite_note-124)[[100]](#cite_note-McConnachie_2014-125)

* [Gabon](/wiki/Gabon "Gabon") and [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo"), the most recent members to join the Commonwealth in 2022, became part of the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") in 2023 and are expected to make their debut in the 2026 edition.[[101]](#cite_note-Commonwealth_Sport_2023-126)
* [Tokelau](/wiki/Tokelau "Tokelau"), a dependency of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), was expected to take part for the first time at the 2010 Games in Delhi but did not do so.[[102]](#cite_note-127) In 2018, Tokelau was noted to be ineligible for the Commonwealth Games until it became affiliated to at least five [international sport federations](/wiki/International_sport_federations "International sport federations").[[103]](#cite_note-RNZ_2018-128)
* [Christmas Island](/wiki/Christmas_Island "Christmas Island") and the [Cocos (Keeling) Islands](/wiki/Cocos_(Keeling)_Islands "Cocos (Keeling) Islands"), both [external territories](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") of Australia like [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"), have not yet sent teams of their own.
* The [Pitcairn Islands](/wiki/Pitcairn_Islands "Pitcairn Islands"), a [British Overseas Territory](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territory "British Overseas Territory"), does not compete due to its small population (around 50 people).[[104]](#cite_note-Pitcairn-129)

Other inhabited territories and [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") within the Commonwealth, such as [Ascension Island](/wiki/Ascension_Island "Ascension Island") and [Tristan da Cunha](/wiki/Tristan_da_Cunha "Tristan da Cunha") (parts of the British Overseas Territory of [Saint Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha](/wiki/Saint_Helena,_Ascension_and_Tristan_da_Cunha "Saint Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha")), [Nevis](/wiki/Nevis "Nevis") (a federal entity of the [Federation of Saint Kitts and Nevis](/wiki/Saint_Kitts_and_Nevis "Saint Kitts and Nevis")), [Rodrigues](/wiki/Rodrigues "Rodrigues") (an [outer island](/wiki/Outer_Islands_of_Mauritius "Outer Islands of Mauritius") of [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius")), and [Zanzibar](/wiki/Zanzibar "Zanzibar") (a semi-autonomous part of Tanzania), are not considered to be separate associated or overseas territories by the Commonwealth[[98]](#cite_note-Commonwealth_of_Nations_2014-123) and so are unlikely to be eligible.

### Rejected participants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Rejected participants")]

Campaigners from the [English county](/wiki/English_county "English county") of [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") asked the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") to allow Cornwall to participate independently in the 2006 Games, but were rejected by the CGF, which stated that "Cornwall is no more than an English county" and advised Cornwall athletes to compete on the [England](/wiki/England "England") team.[[105]](#cite_note-BBC_NEWS_2006-130)[[106]](#cite_note-Harris_2006-131) Cornwall political party [Mebyon Kernow](/wiki/Mebyon_Kernow "Mebyon Kernow") unsuccessfully called for a Cornwall team at the 2022 Games.[[107]](#cite_note-Hart_2022-132)

The [Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus](/wiki/Turkish_Republic_of_Northern_Cyprus "Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus") applied to take part in the 2006 Games, but was rejected due to a lack of [international recognition](/wiki/International_recognition "International recognition").[[105]](#cite_note-BBC_NEWS_2006-130)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### Host city contract

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Host city contract")]

The [1934 British Empire Games](/wiki/1934_British_Empire_Games "1934 British Empire Games"), originally awarded in 1930 to [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg"), were moved to London after South Africa's pre-[apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") government refused to allow participants of colour.[[108]](#cite_note-Gorman2012-133)

The [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games") were originally awarded to [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") on 2 September 2015, at the [CGF](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") General Assembly in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland").[[109]](#cite_note-134) It was reported in February 2017 that Durban may be unable to host the games due to financial constraints. On 13 March 2017, the CGF stripped Durban of their rights to host and reopened the bidding process for the 2022 games.[[110]](#cite_note-135) Many cities from Australia, Canada, England and Malaysia expressed interest to host the games. However, the CGF received only one official bid and that was from [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), England.[[111]](#cite_note-136) On 21 December 2017, [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") was awarded for the 2022 Games as Durban's replacement host.[[112]](#cite_note-137)

A joint bid from 6 cities of the [Victoria State](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)") in Australia was selected to host the [2026 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games"). On 18 July 2023, the Premier of Victoria [Dan Andrews](/wiki/Daniel_Andrews "Daniel Andrews") announced that the state was giving up on hosting the event due a significant increase in event forecast. Initial costs estimated an investment of approximately[A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar")2.6 billion, but the new prospects were resulting to be closer to A$6–7 billion.[[113]](#cite_note-138)[[114]](#cite_note-139) On 18 July 2023, the [Victorian state government](/wiki/Victorian_state_government "Victorian state government") announced it had cancelled its plans to host the games, citing an escalation in its cost projections relative to initial estimations. With no host city, there was a possibility that the games would be postponed to 2027 or cancelled.[[115]](#cite_note-:7-140)[[116]](#cite_note-141)[[117]](#cite_note-:CancelledGuardian-142) However, on 17 September 2024, it was announced that the Scottish Government had agreed to host the 2026 games in [Glasgow](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games").[[118]](#cite_note-143)

### Boycotts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Boycotts")]

Much like the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), the Commonwealth Games have also experienced boycotts:

[Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") boycotted the [1978 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1978_Commonwealth_Games "1978 Commonwealth Games") at [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton") in protest of New Zealand's sporting contacts with [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid")-era South Africa. [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda") also stayed away, in protest of alleged Canadian hostility towards the government of [Idi Amin](/wiki/Idi_Amin "Idi Amin").[[34]](#cite_note-MacintoshHawes1994-35)[[119]](#cite_note-Edmonton-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/1986_Commonwealth_Games_%28Edinburgh%29_boycotting_countries_%28red%29.png/330px-1986_Commonwealth_Games_%28Edinburgh%29_boycotting_countries_%28red%29.png)](/wiki/File:1986_Commonwealth_Games_(Edinburgh)_boycotting_countries_(red).png)

Countries that boycotted the 1986 Games are shaded red

During the [1986 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games") at [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), a majority of the Commonwealth nations staged a boycott, so that the Games appeared to be a whites-only event. Thirty two of the eligible fifty nine countries—largely African, Asian and Caribbean states—stayed away because of the [Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") government's policy of keeping Britain's sporting links with [apartheid South Africa](/wiki/Apartheid_South_Africa "Apartheid South Africa") in preference to participating in the general sporting boycott of that country. Consequently, Edinburgh 1986 witnessed the lowest number of athletes since Auckland 1950.[[120]](#cite_note-145) The boycotting nations were [Antigua and Barbuda](/wiki/Antigua_and_Barbuda "Antigua and Barbuda"), [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), [Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica"), [Gambia](/wiki/The_Gambia "The Gambia"), [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana"), [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"), [Solomon Islands](/wiki/Solomon_Islands "Solomon Islands"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [St. Vincent and the Grenadines](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_and_the_Grenadines "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines"), [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone"), [St. Kitts and Nevis](/wiki/Saint_Kitts_and_Nevis "Saint Kitts and Nevis"), [St. Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucia "Saint Lucia"), [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"), [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"), [Turks and Caicos Islands](/wiki/Turks_and_Caicos_Islands "Turks and Caicos Islands"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia") and [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe").[[121]](#cite_note-146) Bermuda was a particularly late withdrawal, as its athletes appeared in the opening ceremony and in the opening day of competition before the Bermuda Olympic Association decided to formally withdraw.[[122]](#cite_note-147)

### Protests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Protests")]

The [1982 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1982_Commonwealth_Games "1982 Commonwealth Games") in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") took place amid mass protests for [Australian Aboriginal](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal "Australian Aboriginal") rights. The controversial [Joh Bjelke-Petersen](/wiki/Joh_Bjelke-Petersen "Joh Bjelke-Petersen") state government had been repeatedly been challenged by the [Queensland Council for Civil Liberties](/wiki/Queensland_Council_for_Civil_Liberties "Queensland Council for Civil Liberties") over the restrictions it placed on freedom of speech, freedom of association and freedom to protest. The [Government of Queensland](/wiki/Government_of_Queensland "Government of Queensland") did not recognise Aboriginal land rights. Queensland also placed severe legal restrictions on Aboriginal people through the "Aboriginal Act 1971".

Aboriginal activists including [Gary Foley](/wiki/Gary_Foley "Gary Foley") planned mass demonstrations in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") during the week of the games, dubbed the "Stolenwealth Games". In response, Queensland passed "The Commonwealth Games Act 1982" to restrict protests in or near the event. When Aboriginal activists and their supporters marched anyway, hundreds were arrested. The protests were recorded in the documentary "Guniwaya Ngigu".

Further "Stolenwealth Games" protests took place during the [2006 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games") in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") and [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") on the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland").[[123]](#cite_note-148)

## Financial implications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Financial implications")]

The estimated cost of the [2010 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2010_Commonwealth_Games "2010 Commonwealth Games") in Delhi was US$11 billion, according to *[Business Today](/wiki/Business_Today_(India) "Business Today (India)")* magazine.[[124]](#cite_note-149) The initial total budget estimated by the [Indian Olympic Association](/wiki/Indian_Olympic_Association "Indian Olympic Association") in 2003 was US$250 million. In 2010, however, the official total budget soon escalated to an estimated US$1.8 billion, a figure which excluded non-sports-related infrastructure development.[[125]](#cite_note-150) The 2010 Commonwealth Games is reportedly the most expensive Commonwealth Games ever.[[126]](#cite_note-151)

An analysis conducted by [PricewaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers") on the [2002](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games"), [2006](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games"), [2014](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games") and [2018](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") Commonwealth Games found that each dollar spent by governments on operating costs, games venues and athletes' villages generated US$2 for the host city or state economies, with an average of more than 18,000 jobs generated by each of the events.[[127]](#cite_note-152)[[128]](#cite_note-153) Additionally, all four cities enjoyed long-term improvements to transport or other infrastructure through hosting the Games, while some also benefited from the revival of struggling precincts.[[129]](#cite_note-154)

An analysis conducted by [Ernst & Young](/wiki/Ernst_%26_Young "Ernst & Young") found that the [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") generated an estimated economic impact of $2.5 billion, while the venues constructed and upgraded for the Games generated over $60 million in economic benefit annually to the Gold Coast, with the success of the 2018 Commonwealth Games credited with helping Brisbane to secure hosting rights for the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics").[[130]](#cite_note-155)[[131]](#cite_note-156)

## Notable competitors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Notable competitors")]

Lawn bowler [Willie Wood](/wiki/Willie_Wood_(bowler) "Willie Wood (bowler)") from [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") was the first competitor to have competed in seven Commonwealth Games, from 1974 to 2002, a record equalled in 2014 by [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") cyclist [Andrew Roche](/wiki/Andrew_Roche "Andrew Roche").[[132]](#cite_note-157) They have both been surpassed by [David Calvert](/wiki/David_Calvert_(sport_shooter) "David Calvert (sport shooter)") of [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") who in 2018 attended his 11th games.[[133]](#cite_note-158)

[Sitiveni Rabuka](/wiki/Sitiveni_Rabuka "Sitiveni Rabuka") was a Prime Minister of [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"). Beforehand he represented Fiji in shot put, hammer throw, discus and the decathlon at the 1974 [British Commonwealth Games](/wiki/British_Commonwealth_Games "British Commonwealth Games") held in Christchurch, New Zealand.

[Greg Yelavich](/wiki/Greg_Yelavich "Greg Yelavich"), a sports shooter from New Zealand, has won 12 medals in seven games from 1986 to 2010.[[134]](#cite_note-Greg_Yelavich_Olympic_success-159)

Lawn bowler [Robert Weale](/wiki/Robert_Weale "Robert Weale") has represented Wales in 8 Commonwealth Games, 1986–2014, winning 2 gold, 3 silver and 1 bronze.[[135]](#cite_note-160)

Nauruan weightlifter [Marcus Stephen](/wiki/Marcus_Stephen "Marcus Stephen") won twelve medals at the Games between 1990 and 2002, of which seven gold, and was elected [President of Nauru](/wiki/President_of_Nauru "President of Nauru") in 2007. His performance has helped place Nauru (the smallest independent state in the Commonwealth, at 21 km2 (8.1 sq mi) and with a population of fewer than 9,400 in 2011) in twenty-second place on the [all-time Commonwealth Games medal table](/wiki/All-time_Commonwealth_Games_medal_table "All-time Commonwealth Games medal table").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Australian swimmer [Ian Thorpe](/wiki/Ian_Thorpe "Ian Thorpe") has won 10 Commonwealth Games gold medals and 1 silver medal. At the [1998 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1998_Commonwealth_Games "1998 Commonwealth Games") in Kuala Lumpur, he won 4 gold medals. At the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") in Manchester, he won 6 gold medals and 1 silver medal.[[136]](#cite_note-161)

[Chad le Clos](/wiki/Chad_le_Clos "Chad le Clos"), South Africa's most decorated swimmer, has won 18 medals from four Commonwealth Games (2010, 2014, 2018 & 2022), seven of which are gold. At the [2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games") in Glasgow, he won two gold medals, one silver medal, and four bronze medals.[[137]](#cite_note-162) At the [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games") in [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), he won three golds, a silver and a bronze.[[138]](#cite_note-163)

English actor [Jason Statham](/wiki/Jason_Statham "Jason Statham") took part as a diver in the 1990 Commonwealth Games.[[139]](#cite_note-164)

At the [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games") in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), Australian singer [Cody Simpson](/wiki/Cody_Simpson "Cody Simpson") won a gold medal as a swimmer at the [men's 4 × 100 metre freestyle relay](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_4_%C3%97_100_metre_freestyle_relay "Swimming at the 2022 Commonwealth Games – Men's 4 × 100 metre freestyle relay") and a silver at the [men's 4 × 100 metre medley relay](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_4_%C3%97_100_metre_medley_relay "Swimming at the 2022 Commonwealth Games – Men's 4 × 100 metre medley relay").[[140]](#cite_note-165)

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") was the principal broadcaster from 1954 to 2022 apart from the first four editions in 1930, 1934, 1938 and 1950. On 19 December 2025, it is announced that the [2026 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games") in Glasgow will be shown live on [TNT Sports](/wiki/TNT_Sports "TNT Sports") for the first time on paywall television instead on free-to-air television.
